topic.what: Earliest systematic Western medical writings on stomach ailments, framing gastric disorders as humoral (bile) imbalance treated through dietary regimen.
topic.how: Observational case descriptions organized by humoral theory — excess bile causes gastric inflammation, corrected through diet, fasting, and purgation.
topic.why_matters: Established the 2,000-year paradigm that stomach disease is fundamentally dietary, influencing every subsequent Western approach to gastritis until the bacterial revolution.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Degree of influence on subsequent medical thought
characteristics.value: Foundational — dominated Western medicine for 2000 years
characteristics.vs_baseline: First systematic Western gastric medicine; no prior baseline

characteristics.what_it_measures: Whether a complete diagnosis-to-treatment framework is provided
characteristics.evidence: Textual analysis of On Regimen and On Affections

good_for: Understanding origins of Western gastric medicine

novelty: First systematic Western approach to gastric disease as a medical entity
Established dietary regimen as primary gastric therapy
Linked bile disorders to stomach inflammation centuries before biochemistry
Attributed quote 'all disease begins in the gut' shaped medical thought for millennia
open_problems: Attribution uncertainty — multiple authors over centuries
No controlled observation or empirical validation
Difficult to separate original Hippocratic thought from later interpolation
